qa.a: Immunity to a particular pathogen can be acquired in two fundamentally different ways - actively, by the person mounting their own response, or passively, by receiving ready-made antibody - and the distinction matters for vaccination, for prophylaxis and for the immunoglobulin preparations we sometimes give. Active immunity is generated when the individuals own immune system is stimulated by antigen and mounts a response, producing antibody and, crucially, lasting memory; it arises naturally from infection or artificially from vaccination. Its advantages are that it is durable, often lifelong, because of immunological memory, and that it protects against future exposures; its disadvantage is that it takes days to weeks to develop, so it does not protect immediately. Passive immunity, by contrast, is conferred by giving preformed antibody made by another individual or animal, so the recipient is protected immediately without mounting their own response. It occurs naturally when maternal immunoglobulin G crosses the placenta and when antibody passes in breast milk, protecting the newborn, and artificially when we administer antibody preparations. Its advantage is immediate protection; its disadvantage is that it is temporary, lasting only weeks to months until the given antibody is catabolised, and it confers no memory. Immunoglobulin therapy is the clinical use of passive immunity: pooled human immunoglobulin for replacement in antibody deficiency or as immunomodulation in high dose, and specific immunoglobulins - such as those against tetanus, hepatitis B, rabies or the rhesus D antigen - given for immediate post-exposure protection. So active immunity is the persons own memory-bearing, durable response from infection or vaccination, while passive immunity is immediate but temporary protection from preformed antibody, the basis of maternal protection and of the immunoglobulin preparations we give for replacement and post-exposure prophylaxis.

qa.q: What is the immune role of the spleen, and what are the implications of asplenia?
qa.a: The spleen is an important secondary lymphoid organ with distinctive immune functions, and its loss or impairment leaves a characteristic and dangerous susceptibility to infection that the anaesthetist and perioperative physician must recognise and guard against. The spleen has two functionally distinct regions. The red pulp filters the blood, removing aged and abnormal red cells and, importantly, clearing blood-borne micro-organisms, particularly encapsulated bacteria that are difficult for other mechanisms to deal with. The white pulp is lymphoid tissue where immune responses to blood-borne antigens are mounted, and the spleen is a major site of antibody production and of the clearance of antibody-coated cells. It also removes particulate matter and is a reservoir of platelets and, in some species, red cells. The consequence of these functions is that the asplenic or hyposplenic patient - whether after surgical splenectomy, from trauma, or from functional asplenia as in sickle-cell disease - is at lifelong risk of overwhelming, rapidly fatal sepsis, particularly from encapsulated organisms such as the pneumococcus, the meningococcus and Haemophilus, because the filtration and antibody response to these organisms is impaired. This risk is mitigated by vaccination against these encapsulated organisms, ideally before elective splenectomy, by antibiotic prophylaxis, and by prompt treatment of febrile illness. So the spleen filters blood-borne organisms, especially encapsulated bacteria, and mounts antibody responses, so that asplenia confers a lifelong risk of overwhelming sepsis from encapsulated organisms, managed by vaccination, antibiotic prophylaxis and vigilant early treatment of infection.

qa.q: Thank you. An excellent and complete account. Let us stop there. Before we close, here are the take-home messages. First, Immunity has a rapid, non-specific innate arm (barriers, phagocytes, complement, inflammation, pattern-recognition of PAMPs and DAMPs) and a slower, antigen-specific adaptive arm (T cells, B cells and antibody) with memory; the two are interdependent. Second, We routinely breach the innate barriers with lines, tubes and incisions, so asepsis and infection prevention are fundamental; surgery and critical illness drive a biphasic pro-inflammatory then immunosuppressed response. Third, Anaphylaxis is the acute immunological emergency: massive mediator release causing hypotension, bronchospasm and angioedema, presenting under anaesthesia as sudden hypotension and high airway pressures, treated immediately with adrenaline, oxygen and fluids, with tryptase and later allergy testing. Fourth, The leading perioperative anaphylaxis triggers are neuromuscular blockers, antibiotics (beta-lactams) and chlorhexidine, with latex and others; identifying the culprit by tryptase and specialist testing is mandatory to prevent re-exposure. Fifth, Know the immunocompromised (HIV, transplant immunosuppression - continue perioperatively, watch toxicity and infection), autoimmune disease (myasthenia altering relaxant response, rheumatoid airway and spine), and sepsis as a dysregulated immune response managed by source control, antibiotics and resuscitation. Thank you. That concludes the station.
